title: Jonathan Ross interviews Anno Sensei
wikitext:
[]: Jonathan Ross interviews Anno-Sensei             Quote Post by Jayfive » Thu May 26, 2011 1:47 pm Not sure how many of you non-UK bods have seen this: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=csUIy94V2RQ&feature=related Jonathan Ross, UK TV celeb (wiki him, its easier) and big japanophile interviews Anno. 2:40 onwards Yeah, OK so its about Love and Pop and eva just gets a brief mention but hell except for the 'Welcome back sensei' thing youd be hard pushed to find many interviews with Ole Beardy. Especially ones in english. At very very leastyou can have fun spotting some of the cinematic styles of Anno's live stuff with the Anime. BTW this is here because I thought 'everything else' covers 'stuff to do with the shows creator'. If the mods think otherwise feel free to boot this into somewhere else in the forms,

[]:             Quote Post by shinji_ryoji_89 » Thu May 26, 2011 3:16 pm What?! Love and Pop is 110 minutes long! Not 160 minutes! Is there a longer cut available somewhere? Or is it just a mistake on Ross' part? Good, short interview, though with Anno-san. It reminded me of how disturbing and good that film is. Thanks for posting it.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu May 26, 2011 3:16 pm Yeah, seen this before. I think someone else posted it a while ago. It's... interesting.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Thu May 26, 2011 3:42 pm For additional fun, point and laugh at the YT muppets call Ross a racist. If he was a racist we wouldnt have done the show in the first place. I'd recommend the 2 series - it balances stuff about mainstream Japanese culture with some of the more outlandish things with being all 'Hey look at them ker-azy japs!' about it.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu May 26, 2011 6:18 pm Saw this ages ago. Anno with camera on his head is awesome and crazy I love Anno when he is in his artsyfartsy batshit insane mode

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Thu May 26, 2011 6:31 pm Yep, I saw this. Anno's interview was mildly interesting, but Oshii's interview was a lot more fascinating and elucidating. (He thinks Major Kusanagi's body ripping to pieces is very erotic. Also, he thinks he's a reincarnated basset hound.) This was also where I learned of Battle Royale , so I thank Japanorama for that.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Thu May 26, 2011 6:46 pm NAveryW wrote: He thinks Major Kusanagi's body ripping to pieces is very erotic. Also, he thinks he's a reincarnated basset hound.

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Thu May 26, 2011 7:03 pm NAveryW, got a link of that? Now my interests are piqued.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu May 26, 2011 9:47 pm C.A.P. wrote: NAveryW, got a link of that? Now my interests are piqued.

[]:             Quote Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Thu May 26, 2011 11:06 pm NAveryW wrote: (He thinks Major Kusanagi's body ripping to pieces is very erotic. Also, he thinks he's a reincarnated basset hound.)

[]: Re: Jonathan Ross interviews Anno-Sensei             Quote Post by symbv » Fri May 27, 2011 1:19 am Jayfive wrote: Not sure how many of you non-UK bods have seen this: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=csUIy94V2RQ&feature=related

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri May 27, 2011 2:26 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Oh man, I saw that one a while ago, but it got taken down from Youtube. If I recall it correctly - and admittedly that's a big "if" - he also talks about how much he loves computers, and how sometimes when he's using them "I feel like I am the computer". And I think what he says isn't that he thinks he was a basset hound in a past life, but that he wants to be one in a future life. That was priceless. That was the exact point at which I realized Oshii is certifiably nuts (and came to adore him for it). It never hit me that it was part of the same show as the Anno interview, though.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri May 27, 2011 5:46 pm No, it was definitely the same interview. British narrator, mainly focused on GitS . Sounds right. And yeah, no anime film (save perhaps for Pokemon ) has ever been #1 in the US, if we're talking about box-office numbers.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Fri May 27, 2011 7:27 pm NAveryW wrote: I think one of the most interesting parts of Japanorama was when Oshii said that unlike in the United States, mass audiences don't care about animation in Japan. He gave Ghost in the Shell becoming #1 in the US as an indicator. I remembered reading Toshio Okada saying that Ghost in the Shell was never actually #1 in the US, but the advertisers in Japan said it was to drive up their own sales. I felt kind of bad that they apparently tricked the film's director. Though I recently read a(n American) news article from the '90s that said Ghost in the Shell was "a top video rental", so maybe that's what they meant.

[]:             Quote Post by drinian » Fri May 27, 2011 8:46 pm I've heard that during the height of the DVD era (the past decade), more than half of the anime industry's revenue came from outside Japan. But that was a very rough number and I don't recall where I heard it. It makes sense, though.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri May 27, 2011 9:22 pm drinian wrote: I've heard that during the height of the DVD era (the past decade), more than half of the anime industry's revenue came from outside Japan. But that was a very rough number and I don't recall where I heard it. It makes sense, though.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Fri May 27, 2011 9:32 pm drinian wrote: I've heard that during the height of the DVD era (the past decade), more than half of the anime industry's revenue came from outside Japan. But that was a very rough number and I don't recall where I heard it. It makes sense, though.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri May 27, 2011 10:48 pm symbv wrote: That said, I still have a feeling that perhaps the "half the revenue" only applies to the most successful titles -- like Ghibli works for example.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Sat May 28, 2011 9:22 am gatotsu911 wrote: Does it really matter, though? A handful of legendary anime, including Akira , Ghost in the Shell , Escaflowne , Cowboy Bebop , Baccano , and a number of others I can't recall offhand are known to have performed significantly better overseas than in Japan. Western anime fans seem completely oblivious to the fact that their purchases MATTER. Or, at least they did.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sat May 28, 2011 9:41 am That anime industry's attempt to take over foreign markets failed miserably and starting around 2006 or so the focus shifted nigh-entirely to home market and core otaku audience should say it all Or as Jinx put it Anime is going to die? Nope, anime isn’t going anywhere. The one that’s dying is your business, and as a European I only can applaud to this development. If American market was vital to the Japanese, you’d think they would actually do something about it instead of producing shows that mainly appeal to the niche audiences and the domestic market back in Asia. noitaminA shows are excellent, but there is no way they would sell in the West – at least not enough to fill the current capacity the R1 industry has built up.

Article: Jonathan Ross interviews Anno Sensei
 isn’t going anywhere. The one that’s dying is your business, and as a European I only can applaud to this development. If American market was vital to the Japanese, you’d think they would actually do something about it instead of producing shows that mainly appeal to the niche audiences and the domestic market back in Asia. noitaminA shows are excellent, but there is no way they would sell in the West – at least not enough to fill the current capacity the R1 industry has built up.
